sections.about: LTIFR for employees measures lost time injuries per million person-hours and is reported under BRSR Principle 3, Essential Indicator 11 on safety-related incidents. LTIFR is calculated as lost time injuries multiplied by 1,000,000 divided by total hours worked. A lower value indicates better performance.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: Tata improved steadily from 0.87 in FY23 to 0.49 in FY24 and 0.39 in FY25, but remained last among peers in each year. SAIL led FY23 and FY24 at 0.02 in both years, while Jindal became the FY25 leader with 0. JSW improved sharply to 0.11 in FY24 but slipped to 0.25 in FY25. Jindal’s FY25 outcome is supported by ISO 45001 coverage across locations, Zero Harm orientation, HIRA/JSA, safety audits and proactive hazard reporting. Tata’s reduction appears linked to ISO 45001-based EHSMS, risk matrix updates, fatality-risk control, safety leadership, contractor safety audits and digital incident systems, but the absolute LTIFR remains above peers.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2024-25, Tata ranked 4th at 0.39. The best performer was Jindal at 0, so a percentage gap is not meaningful; the absolute gap is 0.39 per million person-hours. Tata’s ranking did not improve despite year-on-year reduction, because peers also reported very low employee LTIFR and Jindal reached zero.
sections.targets: Tata states an aim for zero harm, and Jindal states Zero Harm and industry leadership in safety and health performance.
sections.comparabilityNotes: Tata FY2022-23 is consolidated, while other years are standalone.
